transcript.clean_text: It was time for day three, but stage two of the Tour de Romandie. Beautiful weather conditions, 16 degrees, a little bit of wind, and the course taking us from Echelon back again to Echelon. 2 and 1/2 thousand meters of elevation again. Lots of multiple changes of direction. Three passages through the finish line and a flat fast finish. Only one categorized climb coming midway through the climb of La Pra. But another lighting day for the field in this beautiful race. They set out in the morning under these glorious conditions in Echelon, which is a municipality in the district of Gland Vaud, and the shortly after the start a four-man breakaway went clear. Baptiste Planckaert, Tom Scully, Diego Lopez, and Nils Brun representing the Swiss national team. And meanwhile, unsurprisingly, the workload at the front of the peloton was being done by the yellow and black of Jumbo-Visma looking after the lead of Rohan Dennis. The ultimate passage of the line taking them into one last loop. The breakaway continued to work hard, but then with 38 coats to go, on one of the numerous climbs that peppered the course, the first to crack was Baptiste Planckaert of Intermarché-Wanty-Gobert. And shortly afterwards, Diego Lopez could hold on to the breakaway no longer. That left two riders out in front, Tom Scully and Nils Brun. Diego Lopez was caught by the peloton. UAE Team Emirates also assisting with the pace making. Jan Polanc doing a lot of long pulls on the front and clearly the strongest out in front was the Latvian road champion, Tom Scully, and pulling as hard as he could with Brun for company, but the lead was to come to an end with just over 20 kilometers to go. It was Ineos Grenadiers riding exceptionally hard on the front. Laurens De Plus, the Belgian, setting an infernal tempo on one of these long drags here which really did thin out the peloton. Look at the shape there. Very, very tough UK's also doing a lot of the work. A Brandon Rivera. They'd be looking after Ethan Hayter for the ensuing sprint. Another Ethan couldn't quite hold on the pace. Ethan Vernon struggled on the climb and was dropped leaving a group of around 70 riders to battle it out for the finish. It was to be a chaotic sprint. The wind dropped a little bit. Couple of teams moving to the front in the finale. Well placed on the left-hand side there, the race leader, Rohan Dennis. And then with an absolutely humongous long turn, it was Magnus Sheffield who set up his team leader for the sprint, Ethan Hayter. He opened up his sprint very, very early indeed. And closing fast was Fernando Gaviria. And on the inside as well, Jon Aberasturi. But in the end, Ethan Hayter took his first ever World Tour road win and with it silencing his critics. Disappointment yesterday turned into delight today. And what a fine team effort it was by the Ineos Grenadiers. And well, we're running out of superlatives for Magnus Sheffield placing his man perfectly. He rode with patience, precision, and power to steal another win making it two wins from three in the Tour de Romandie so far. Also, look on the right-hand side there, a very, very fast finishing Alexandr Vlasov from Bora-Hansgrohe, the best of the GC contenders. But look at what it meant from the man from London. Ethan Hayter took the win ahead of Jon Aberasturi. Uh Fernando Gaviria in third. Then we have Vlasov, Quinn, Hermans, Hirt, O'Connor, Grossschartner, and rounding out the top 10, Stephen Kruijswijk. So, smiles all round for Ethan Hayter. It's been an up-and-down journey so far in this race, but today in the sunshine, he was clearly the strongest. So, heading into stage three, Rohan Dennis leads this race ahead of Felix Grossschartner and Mauro Schmid in third place. Then we have Teuns, Are U So Hon Lee, Bevin, McNulty, and the rest. So, another day in green for the Australian, Rohan Dennis, who is looking very, very strong indeed as we head into the next road stage of this race. 165 rolling kilometers from Valbroie to Valbroie. Make sure you do not miss it.
